description: One conventional unit of a language is a 'phelui' (word), with a form and a shared lexical or grammatical use. The noun can also refer to particular wording when several words are gathered as 'lo phelui' (words). 'shemui' (utter) produces a bounded stretch of wording, while a word remains the unit being used or discussed.
articulatory_notes: Bring the lips close for unvoiced bilabial 'ph' and let the airflow open into middle-height front 'e'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for 'l' and round into stressed high-back 'u'. Keep the last syllable distinct while unrounding and moving the tongue into high-front 'i'.
usage_notes: Exact spoken words can stand inside 'shola ... sholo'. In metalinguistic use, a Phi form remains lowercase and can be discussed as a 'phelui' without turning its English gloss into part of the Phi sentence.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Right Speech is practised through actual choices of wording, not through a label applied afterward. 'phelui' lets one such choice be examined directly.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A single word can assign agency or place a person inside a category. It may also hide a source. Naming the unit makes those choices available for revision.

semantic_domains.cognition: a conventional form recalled and understood as one unit
semantic_domains.communication: a basic lexical or grammatical unit used in expression
